title: Labels and input controls

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific HTML: Semantic and Accessible Web Documents draft for Labels and input controls; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: HTML Living Standard; living standard accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to Labels and input controls
sources.claim: A label associates an accessible name with its form control through nesting or matching for and id values.

sources.locator: Web Content Accessibility Guidelines 2.2; W3C Recommendation 2023; provisions applicable to Labels and input controls
sources.claim: A label associates an accessible name with its form control through nesting or matching for and id values.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A label associates an accessible name with its form control through nesting or matching for and id values.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A label for=email points to one input id=email while help and errors are connected separately. Your goal is to create a keyboard-operable form whose controls have persistent accessible names. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Paragraph and inline meaning; Relative and absolute URLs; Form submission model. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: A label associates an accessible name with its form control through nesting or matching for and id values. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Placeholder text is not a persistent label, duplicate ids break association, and custom controls need equivalent semantics and state. This draft therefore rejects: Placeholder text is an adequate replacement for a visible label.

includes: Associate visible labels with suitable native input, textarea, select and button controls.
Create a keyboard-operable form whose controls have persistent accessible names.
excludes: Placeholder text is not a persistent label, duplicate ids break association, and custom controls need equivalent semantics and state.

outcomes: Create a keyboard-operable form whose controls have persistent accessible names.

examples.title: Labels and input controls: concrete case
examples.situation: A label for=email points to one input id=email while help and errors are connected separately.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: A label for=email points to one input id=email while help and errors are connected separately.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Give every control a persistent programmatic label; placeholders may offer examples but do not replace labels.
examples.result: The result follows because Give every control a persistent programmatic label; placeholders may offer examples but do not replace labels.

Step 3 — Reject the misconception: Placeholder text is an adequate replacement for a visible label.
